title_slide: Python for Data Science: Why Your Setup Matters More Than Code

slides.heading: The Two Ways to Learn Python
bullet_points: Additive approach: collect syntax rules until overwhelmed
Structural approach: learn thinking first, code second
Data science demands structural thinking from day one
slides.speaker_note: Set the tension: most tutorials teach the wrong way. We're breaking that pattern.

slides.heading: Why Environment Setup Fails (And What It Costs)
bullet_points: Bad setup = unreproducible results that fail months later
Version conflicts and silent failures multiply
Global installs create cascade problems
Most 'failures' aren't Python—they're foundation issues
slides.speaker_note: Tell a story: my subtitle generation broke because a library updated silently. The code was fine. The environment wasn't.

slides.heading: Virtual Environments: Your Non-Negotiable Foundation
bullet_points: Isolate each project's dependencies completely
Never install globally again
One command: source env/bin/activate
Reproducibility becomes automatic, not aspirational
slides.speaker_note: Walk through the 15-minute setup. Emphasize: this prevents months of debugging later.

slides.heading: Computational Thinking: Four Parts
bullet_points: Decomposition: break big problems into smaller pieces
Pattern recognition: notice what steps repeat
Abstraction: write it once, reuse everywhere
Algorithm design: sequence the logic clearly
slides.speaker_note: This is how you think like a programmer. Syntax teaching doesn't cover it, but every line of code you write should reflect it.

slides.heading: Your First Program: Data, Not Hello World
bullet_points: Read structured data (list of dictionaries)
Process with loops (the pattern you'll repeat forever)
Save results reproducibly (JSON, not terminal output)
This is already a real program, not a toy exercise
slides.speaker_note: Show the code. Emphasize: this contains the structure of production systems. Only the scale changes.

slides.heading: Error Messages Are Your Teacher
bullet_points: Read the error type: KeyError, ValueError, IndexError
Identify exactly what went wrong
Fix it and run again immediately
That cycle is 80% of all programming
slides.speaker_note: Most people quit on their first error. Success is just reading errors and fixing them repeatedly. Build this habit now.

slides.heading: Your Foundation Is Ready
bullet_points: ✓ Isolated environment with no version conflicts
✓ Mental model for decomposing any problem
✓ Your first working program saving real output
✓ A way to debug and learn from error messages
Everything after scales on this foundation
slides.speaker_note: Close strong. You don't have syntax memorized. You have something better. And that's what compounds into real skill.
